coredata.dc:title: Green one-step synthesis of carbon quantum dots from orange peel for fluorescent detection of Escherichia coli in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xuetao
Li, Yanxiao
Xu, Yiwei
Gan, Ziyu
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Li, Yahui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared by a green one-step approach was used for sensitive and selective assay of Escherichia coli O157: H7 (E. coli). CQDs was synthesized from orange peel as a carbon source via a microwave-assisted method. The CQDs displayed strong green fluorescence under excitation wavelength of 420 nm. A fluorescent probe (CQDs-MNPs) for E. coli was fabricated based on CQDs labeled with aptamer (aptamer-CQDs) and magnetic nanoparticles labeled with complementary DNA (cDNA-MNPs). Fluorescent intensity of the CQDs-MNPs was decreased with addition of E. coli. The linearity between fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentration was used for developing a fluorescent method with detecting range of 500–106 CFU/mL and detection limit of 487 CFU/mL. Milk samples contaminated by E. coli were analyzed by this method, and the results agreed with that achieved by plate-counting methods. This fluorescent probe exhibits great potential in guaranteeing food quality and safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Escherichia coli O157:H7
Magnetic nanoparticles
Orange peel

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Article(s) from the Special Issue on 4th International Symposium on Phytochemicals in Medicine and Food by Dr. Xiao, Dr. Zou, Dr. Lu, Dr. Ismail

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Culture and determination of E. Coli
Assay of E. Coli in milk samples stored for different time
Characterization of aptamer-CQDs and cDNA-MNPs conjugates
Specificity and stability of the fluorescent probes
Assay of E. Coli in milk samples stored for different time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of CQDs.
(a) TEM images of CQDs, (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, (c) XRD pattern of CQDs, (d) XPS spectrum of CQDs, (e) EDS mapping images of CQDs showing the distribution of C, N and O.
(a) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs (insets: images of CQDs under (left) visible light and (right) ultraviolet light of 365 nm), (b) Fluorescent lifetime of CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, aptamer-CQDs, MNPs and cDNA-MNPs, (b) Zeta potential of CQDs and aptamer-CQDs, (c) Zeta potential of MNPs and cDNA-MNPs, (d) Fluorescent intensity of aptamer-CQDs hybridized with different volumes of cDNA-MNPs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs-MNPs in the presence of E. coli at different concentrations (0, 500, 103, 5 × 103, 104, 5 × 104, 105, 5 × 105 and 106 CFU/mL), (b) The linear plot between fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentration.
(a) Selectivity investigation of CQDs-MNPs probe in presence of blank, E. coli, Lb. acidophilus, Lc. lactis, B. cereus, and S. aureus.

head.ce:title: Green one-step synthesis of carbon quantum dots from orange peel for fluorescent detection of Escherichia coli in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared by a green one-step approach was used for sensitive and selective assay of Escherichia coli O157: H7 (E. coli). CQDs was synthesized from orange peel as a carbon source via a microwave-assisted method. The CQDs displayed strong green fluorescence under excitation wavelength of 420 nm. A fluorescent probe (CQDs-MNPs) for E. coli was fabricated based on CQDs labeled with aptamer (aptamer-CQDs) and magnetic nanoparticles labeled with complementary DNA (cDNA-MNPs). Fluorescent intensity of the CQDs-MNPs was decreased with addition of E. coli. The linearity between fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentration was used for developing a fluorescent method with detecting range of 500–106 CFU/mL and detection limit of 487 CFU/mL. Milk samples contaminated by E. coli were analyzed by this method, and the results agreed with that achieved by plate-counting methods. This fluorescent probe exhibits great potential in guaranteeing food quality and safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Milk with rich protein, fat, vitamin and mineral content is considered an important part of human diet due to its overall nutritional and health implication(Garcia, Osburn, & Cullor, 2019). However, milk is easy to be contaminated by many bacteria including pathogenic ones during production, processing, transportation or storage. O157: H7 of Escherichia coli (E. coli) is regarded as one of the most dangerous food-borne pathogens (Dell'Orco, Gusmara, Loiacono, Gugliotta, Albonico, Mortarino, et al., 2019). It can cause a variety of diseases (such as bloody diarrhea, kidney failure and even death) even at a very low concentration via milk (Dhull, Kaur, Jain, Mishra, Singh, Ganju, et al., 2019). It is necessary to monitoring E. coli in milk to guarantee food quality and safety.

Various methods for E. coli are mainly based on colony culture(Mortari & Lorenzelli, 2014), polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Macori, McCarthy, Burgess, Fanning, & Duffy, 2019), and enzyme-linked immune-sorbent assay (ELISA)(Mortari & Lorenzelli, 2014). Conventional culture-based methods usually include morphological observation, biochemical screening and serological test. Some of the testing results are easily affected by the colony culture environment. These processes are relatively laborious and time-consuming (more than 7 days for identification)(Shijia, Nuo, Zhao, Congcong, & Zhouping, 2014). Methods based on polymerase chain reaction (PCR) have the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity. But the methods may require precise processing (cell lysis, DNA extraction, purification, and amplification) and they are subjected to some interfering substances (Sáenz, Roldan, Junca, & Arbeli, 2019). Enzyme-linked immune-sorbent assays based on specific interactions between antigen and antibody possess several advantages, such as simple processing, high sensitivity, or good potential for applications. However, they may have drawbacks including cross-reactivity with closely related antigens, and low affinity of some antibodies to their target (Zhang, Yan, Yang, Yu, & Wei, 2017). Therefore, further development of a rapid, sensitive and selective method is still a hot issue for E. coli detection.

Fluorescent methods for E. coli are investigated widely because of their high sensitivity and simplicity (Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Nayak, Mehta, Kim, & Deep, 2017). One of the most significant aspects for fluorescent methods is fluorescent probe generally fabricated by organic fluorescent materials and nanomaterials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterials has been developed as fluorescent probes in food analysis(Y. Wang & Hu, 2015). They have several advantages over fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, such as excellent fluorescent properties, high stability, and low toxicity. The key to prepare CQDs is the synthesized methods and raw materials. Recently, green one-step methods for preparing CQDs attracted great attention because of their simplicity, time saving, non-pollution and low cost. It was reported that papaya and watermelon juice as carbon precursors were used to synthesize carbon quantum dots by using hydrothermal method (Lu, Duan, Song, Tan, & Zhou, 2018; N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016). These approaches are green and simple but might be time-consuming (more than 3 h)(Lu, Duan, Song, Tan, & Zhou, 2018). Simple, rapid and environmentally friendly preparation of CQDs still remains a challenging without using organic chemicals and a lot of time.

Microwave-assisted method was conducted to synthesize CQDs with orange peel as carbon precursors and CQDs-based nanoprobe was fabricated for E. coli. The microwave-assisted method attracted more and more attention because of its simple and time-saving advantages (Chandra, Mahto, Chowdhuri, Das, & Sahu, 2017). Orange peel, a by-product of orange processing, is rich in soluble and insoluble carbohydrate. In addition, it has many functional components, such as vitamins, mineral substances, and flavonoids content. Thus it is considered to be fantastic a carbon precursor. Those rich compositions can make the synthesized CQDs have good physical and chemical properties (such as excellent fluorescence, water-solubility and biocompatibility). Therefore, the study explores a novel way to synthesize CQDs. CQDs-magnetic nanoparticles (CQDs-MNPs) probes were formed to develop a method for rapid and sensitive detection of E. coli.

The oligonucleotide (aptamer) specifically binding to E. coli O157:H7 and complementary DNA to aptamer (cDNA) were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Company Limited (Shanghai, China). The sequence of aptamer and cDNA was 5′-NH2– ATCAAATGTG CAGATATCAA GACGATTTGT ACAAGATCCA TGCTG AGGTG GTCATAGCTG ATCCTACC −3′ and 5′- NH2– TTTCCGGACGCTTATG CCTTGCCATCTACAGAGCAGGTGTGACGG-3′, respectively (Díaz-Amaya, Lin, Deering, & Stanciu, 2019). Ethylene glycol, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 M, 7.4) and Tris were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other reagents (sodium hydroxide (NaOH), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)- 3- ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), etc.) were obtained from Sinopham Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Shanghai, China). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth medium and agar powder were obtained from Beijing Aoboxing Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). The reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. All of solutions were obtained using ultrapure water (electric resistance ≥ 18.2 MΩ·cm) unless mentioned. The aptamer stock solution (100 µM) and cDNA stock solution (100 µM) were prepared using PBS and stored at −18 °C. Orange and pasteurized milk were purchased from a local supermarket, and the milk was stored in plastic containers at 4 °C used within four days. Lactobacillus (Lb.) acidophilus, Lactococcus (Lc.) lactis, and Bacillus (B.) cereus were provided from Fermentation Laboratory of the School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University. Staphylococcus (S.) aureus and E. coli O157: H7 were provided from Food Quality and Safety Laboratory of the School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by JEM- 2100 (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were obtained using a powder X-ray diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250, Thermo scientific) equipped with a monochromatic microspot x-ray beam was employed. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping and spectrum were obtained by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDAX, USA). Zeta potential data were measured by laser particle analyzer (Litesizer 500, Anton-paar Austria). UV–Vis spectra were obtained by a Rayleigh UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Company. Limited, China). The fluorescent lifetime was determined by QuantaMaster™ 40 Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometers (PTI, USA). The fluorescent spectra were recorded on F-98 spectrofluorometer (Lengguang Technology Company, China) and a portable spectrofluorometer assembled in our lab.

The synthesis of CQDs was according to our previous researches (Hu, Shi, Shi, Zou, Arslan, Zhang, et al., 2019). Orange peel was thoroughly cleaned by clear water and dried naturally. The dry orange peel was grounded into powder. The powder (1 g) was mixed with 10 mL of ethylene glycol, and then heated in a domestic microwave oven (900 W, Midea, China) for 1 min. Solution turned into dark brown indicating the formation of CQDs. Then brown solution was purified by centrifuging at 15000 rpm and using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff = 1000) to remove the insoluble substance and small molecules. The CQDs was dried and stored at 4 °C for further investigation. Fig. 1 displays the schematic illustration for preparation of CQDs from orange peel. The synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) was described in supplementary materials(Shijia, Nuo, Zhao, Congcong, & Zhouping, 2014).

Preparation of CQDs-MNPs referred to previous study(Shijia, Nuo, Zhao, Congcong, & Zhouping, 2014). Briefly, CQDs solution (200 µL, 1 mg/mL) and MNPs solution (200 µL, 1.5 mg/mL) were activated in EDC-NHS solution (200 µL) containing EDC (80 mM) and NHS (40 mM). After slightly shaking for 30 min, the activated CQDs was covalently coupled with 1 OD amino-modified aptamer forming aptamer-CQDs. Similarly, the activated MNPs was covalently linked to 1 OD amino-modified cDNA forming cDNA-MNPs. After gentle stirring for 1 h, Tris solution was used to passivize the unreacted carboxyl on the surface of CQDs and MNPs. After 2 h, aptamer-QDs and cDNA-MNPs were mixed and placed for 60 min at 37 °C to cause DNA hybridization, followed by centrifugation to obtain the specific probe (CQDs-MNPs). The centrifugation sediment (CQDs-MNPs) was dissolved in PBS.

Quantum yield of CQDs was determined by using a relative method by taking Quinine sulphate (QS) as standard (N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016). CQDs solution and QS 0.1 M H2SO4 solution were prepared by keeping the absorbance values less than 0.05. Then the fluorescent intensity of all the samples was measured under excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The quantum yields of CQDs are measured according to the following equation:
Q x
=
Q st
A st
/
A x
I x
/
I st
η 2
x
/
η 2
st

Where Q, A and I represent the quantum yield, absorbance and fluorescent intensity, respectively. Qst is equal to 54%; η is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for both solvents, ηst/ηx = 1); The subscript st and subscript × denote to QS and CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture and determination of E. Coli
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli strain was recovered and cultured in LB broth medium (1 g tryptone, 0.5 g yeast extract, 1 g NaCl and 100 mL distilled water, pH 7.4) under aerobic conditions at 37 °C. After reaching stationary phase, E. coli culture solution (3 mL) was centrifuged (5000 rpm, 10 min) and washed with PBS to obtain E. coli cells. Finally, E. coli were suspended in 3 mL of PBS to obtain E. coli stock solution. E. coli stock solution was serially 5-fold diluted to prepare E. coli suspension at concentrations from 0 to 106 CFU/mL (colony- forming units per milliliter) for further experiments. The concentration of E. coli stock solution was also determined by the plate-counting method.
To investigate the selectivity of CQDs-MNPs, E. coli, Lb. acidophilus, Lc. lactis, B. cereus, and S. aureus were also cultured in LB broth medium similar with culture of E. coli.

The amount of CQDs-MNPs and reaction time were optimized to ensure the optimal detection effect. Different concentrations of CQDs-MNPs were measured and the optimal concentration was selected according to the fluorescent intensity (FL intensity, F). The reaction time (1 ~ 60 min) was optimized in the presence of E. coli (104 CFU/mL).

After parameter optimization, CQDs-MNPs was added to a series of E. coli suspension prepared in Section 2.4 and incubated at 37 °C with continuous stirring. The E. coli concentration in mixed solution was 0, 500, 103, 5 × 103, 104, 5 × 104, 105, 5 × 105, and 106 CFU/mL, respectively. Thirty minutes later, CQDs-MNPs was separated by a magnetic field and subsequently dissolved in 300 μL of PBS. The solution was measured by the spectrofluorometer to obtain the fluorescent spectra. The linear relationship between E. coli concentration and corresponding fluorescent intensity was investigated. A calibration curve was established based on the linear relationship for detection of E. coli. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), limit of detection (LOD) for E. coli was calculated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of E. Coli in milk samples stored for different time
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli were added into milk and mixed thoroughly to prepare milk samples contaminated by E. coli with 500 CFU/mL. The contaminated samples were stored at room temperature for 0, 8, 16, 20, and 24 h. Finally, five kinds of milk sample were obtained to investigate the accuracy of this method. Milk samples under different storage time were centrifuged at 5,000 rpm for 20 min, washed with PBS and suspended in PBS. Those sample extracting solutions were detected with CQDs-MNPs according to Section 2.5. Fresh milk samples were used as negative controls and processed in an identical manner. The E. coli concentration in milk samples was measured by plotting fluorescent intensity into the calibration curve. Coefficients of variation (CVs) were used to express the accuracy and reproducibility of this assay.

The plate-counting method was also employed to evaluate detection results obtained by this method. The sample extracting solution was serially diluted with the sterile PBS, and 200 μL of the diluent was plated on the LB agar plates (Tryptone (10 g/L), Yeast extract (5 g/L), NaCl (10 g/L), and agar (20 g/L)). After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C, the bacterial colonies were counted to calculate the concentration of E. coli. A significance statistical analysis was performed between this fluorescent method and the plate-counting method.

CQDs was prepared by microwave-assisted method with orange peel as carbon sources and ethylene glycol as passivation reagents. And the CQDs was characterized by TEM images, XRD pattern, FT-IR spectrum, XPS spectra, EDS spectrum and EDS mapping (Fig. 2 ). TEM has been used extensively to characterize the size and morphology of the CQDs. Fig. 2a displays that the CQDs is well dispersed and exhibits spherical shape with diameter ranging from 3 ~ 5 nm. The average particle diameter of CQDs was about 4.2 nm. The high-resolution TEM image displays that the lattice plane of the CQDs is 0.26 nm interplanar distance (inset in Fig. 2a).

The functional groups on the surface of CQDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The FT-IR absorption bands of CQDs were at 3416, 2936, 2428, 1648, 1349, 1151, and 1082 cm−1. The peak at 3416 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of primary amino group (NH) or hydroxyl group (OH). The band at 2936 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibration. The peaks at 2428 and 1648 cm−1 are attributed to carboxyl CO stretching or secondary amide CO stretching (Chandra, Mahto, Chowdhuri, Das, & Sahu, 2017). The band centered at 1349 cm−1 is detected for the stretching of CN(N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016). The characteristic peaks at 1151 and 1082 cm−1 reflect the CO bond stretching. The FT-IR results reveal that CQDs contains abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs, such as carboxyl and amino groups. These groups demonstrate the excellent water-solubility and biocompatibility of the CQDs. XRD analysis was conducted to investigate the crystallinity of CQDs (Fig. 2c). The XRD pattern of CQDs shows a diffraction peaks centered at about 12° and 30° (2θ), which correspond to amorphous carbon in orange peel(Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014).

XPS and EDS experiments were carried out to explore element composition and element distribution of CQDs. The XPS spectrum shows three predominant peaks at 286, 399, and 532 eV, which are associated with C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2d). The relative atomic percentages of them were 32.95% (C), 1.57% (N) and 66.48% (O), respectively (Table S1). The C1s scan spectrum shows two peaks at 284.8 (CC) and 286.05 eV(CN) (Fig. S1a). The 399 eV of peak in N1s spectrum is ascribed to N in a graphite-like structure or OC-N group (Fig. S1b). The two peaks at 530.8 and 532.1 eV from the O1s spectrum are assigned to CO and C-OH/COC groups, respectively (Fig. S1c) (Song, Wu, Wang, Liu, Song, Li, et al., 2019). The results indicate that CQDs mainly contains carbon(C), and oxygen (O) elements, with low nitrogen (N). The EDS experiment was performed for further analyzing chemical composition of CQDs (Fig. S1d and Fig. 2e). EDS spectrum in Fig. S1d also displays that the CQDs is mainly consisted of C, N and O elements, and the content of N is very low (Dadigala, Bandi, Gangapuram, & Guttena, 2017). Element mapping image in Fig. 1e displays the uniform distribution of C, N and O element on surface of CQDs. The results of element analysis are in agreement with that obtained by XPS analysis. TEM image, FT-IR spectrum and XRD pattern of MNPs are displayed and described in Fig. S2. All the results of characterization demonstrate the successful synthesis of CQDs and MNPs.

Fluorescent emission spectra, fluorescent life and fluorescent quantum yields of CQDs were explored (Fig. 3 ). Emission fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths showed that the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were 420 nm and 522 nm (Fig. 3a). The CQDs possessed strong green fluorescence with an emission peak at ~ 520 nm. The emission peak exhibited unnoticeable shift under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 ~ 450 nm. The fluorescence intensity gradually enhanced with the increase of excitation wavelength increased (350, 360, 370, 380, 390, 400, 410 and 420 nm). And then the fluorescent intensity was reduced as the excitation wavelength increased again (420, 430, 440 and 450 nm). The fluorescent intensity at 522 nm reached the maximum with the excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Fig. 3a). The excitation-dependent emission behavior of CQDs was also observed in other study(N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016). The photographic images show that the CQDs solution is pale yellow under visible light but exhibits strong green fluorescence under irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet (insets in Fig. 3a). The strong fluorescence is attributed to the radiative recombination of the electrons and holes confined to their surface (Hu, Shi, Shi, Zou, Tahir, Holmes, et al., 2019).

Fig. 3b exhibits the fluorescent decay curve of CQDs excited at 481 nm and monitored at 522 nm. The lifetime was calculated to be 2.99 ns after fitting. The fluorescent decay curve displays that the initial intensity is decreased rapidly in a short time and finally reaches to relaxation slowly and stably. The fluorescent curve was fitted by a single exponential to calculate fluorescent lifetime. The single exponential equation is y = y0 + A exp (-t/T), where y is the fluorescence intensity at time t, y0 is the intensity parameter to compensate, A is the decay time constant, t is the decay time, T is the fluorescent lifetime.

The quantum yield of CQDs was measured at 360 nm using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard. The quantum yield was calculated to be 16.2%, which was higher than other CQDs with green fluorescence (Chandra, Mahto, Chowdhuri, Das, & Sahu, 2017; Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014). The performance of optical characterization demonstrates that the CQDs exhibits excellent fluorescent properties. The fluorescent characteristics(fluorescent emission wavelength, fluorescent lifetime, fluorescent quantum yields and fluorescent stability) were comparable to CQDs from other food materials (Lu, Duan, Song, Tan, & Zhou, 2018; N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016). The CQDs synthesis using orange peel as raw materials are green and low-cost because orange peel is the by-product of orange processing. The microwave-assisted method for preparation of CQDs is rapid with less than 5 min. The approach for synthesis of CQDs is rapid, green and low-cost. Previous research demonstrates that CQDs synthesized from food materials are low-toxic and promising for the applications(Lu, Duan, Song, Tan, & Zhou, 2018; Yang, Cao, Luo, Lu, Wang, Wang, et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of aptamer-CQDs and cDNA-MNPs conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugates of CQDs with aptamer (aptamer-CQDs) and MNPs with cDNA (cDNA-MNPs) were investigated by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 4 a) and Zeta potential analyzer (Fig. 4b and 4c). The absorption spectrum of CQDs displays an absorption peak at 300 nm ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC (Fig. 4a). A new absorption peak (~250 nm) appeared after incubated with amino-modified aptamer. This new peak is originated from aptamer because the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) has UV–Vis absorption peaks at around 260 nm. Zeta potential curve displays that the CQDs is negatively charged (-31.2 mV) owing to –COOH groups on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 4b). The zeta potential value was positively shifted to −20.6 mV after forming aptamer-CQDs. These results verify that CQDs has been successfully functionalized with aptamer.

Absorption peak was not observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of MNPs. After coupling with cDNA, cDNA-MNPs exhibited absorption peak at 240 nm which was attributed to cDNA (Fig. 4c). The zeta potential value of MNPs (-49.6 mV) suggests that the surface of MNPs is negatively charged for the carboxyl groups on the surface. As expected, the zeta potential was positively shifted from −49.6 mV to −24.1 mV after forming cDNA-MNPs. The results of UV–Vis spectra and zeta potential data prove that cDNA-MNPs complexes are prepared.

The dosage of cDNA-MNPs solution was optimized in fabrication of fluorescent probes (CQDs-MNPs). The optimization according to the variation in fluorescent intensity was carried out by adding various volumes of cDNA-MNPs solution to 100 μL of aptamer-CQDs solution. The fluorescent intensity at 522 nm was firstly increased then decreased along with the increase in dosage of cDNA-MNPs (Fig. 4d). It reached the maximum value by adding 100 μL of cDNA-MNPs solution. A small amount of CQDs-MNPs was formed and the fluorescent intensity was low for there was not enough amount of cDNA-MNPs. The amount of CQDs-MNPs and fluorescent intensity were increased as cDNA-MNPs increased. They can reach the maximum when the concentration of aptamer-CQDs and cDNA-MNPs is matched. However, the fluorescent intensity of the CQD-MNPs was decreased due to the excessive cDNA-MNPs. That might be because the rest of cDNA-MNPs block some of the fluorescent signal(Shijia, Nuo, Zhao, Congcong, & Zhouping, 2014).

The amount of CQDs-MNPs and incubation time were optimized before E. coli assay (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. S3a, CQDs-MNPs solution at concentration of 1.5 mg/mL showed the highest intensity. The fluorescent intensity of the CQDs-MNPs probes was firstly decreased and then remained stable with time prolonged (Fig. S3b). The fluorescent signal reached equilibrium at incubation time of 40 min. Therefore, the optimal concentration of CQDs-MNPs was 1.5 mg/mL and the reaction time was 40 min during experiments.

The CQDs-MNPs probe was used for developing a fluorescent method for assessment of E. coli (Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5a shows that the fluorescent spectra with different concentration of E. coli (0, 500, 103, 5 × 103, 104, 5 × 104, 105, 5 × 105, and 106 CFU/mL). The fluorescent intensity (FL intensity, F) at 522 nm was decreased gradually with increase of E. coli concentration. Aptamer-CQDs was separated from cDNA-MNPs and then specifically absorbed onto the surface of E. coli. Finally, the amount of CQDs-MNPs was declined leading to the decrease in fluorescent intensity. There was a good linear relationship between fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentration in the range of 500 CFU/mL- 106 CFU/mL (Fig. 5b). The linear equation wass F522 = 933.433–139.355 LgQ with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.977, where F522 represented the FL Intensity at 522 nm, Q was the E. coli concentration (CFU/mL). The limit of detection for E. coli was 487 CFU/mL, displaying a high sensitivity (signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1). Dependent E. coli solutions for each concentration were detected and the coefficient of variation (CV) was less than 4.3% (n = 11). The results demonstrate the fluorescent method exhibits high sensitivity for E. coli detection.

The linear range and limit of detection in this study were comparable to that in most of researches(Table S2) (Chen, Huang, Li, Shan, Lai, & Xiong, 2016; Liu, Lu, Xu, & Wang, 2015; C. Wang, Xing, Zhang, Yuan, Xu, Liu, et al., 2019; N. Wang, Wang, Guo, Yang, Chen, & Wang, 2016; Zhang, Yan, Yang, Yu, & Wei, 2017). This may attribute to magnetic nanoparticles which can separate target or signaling molecules from complex samples. The fabricated fluorescent probe (CQDs-MNPs) can eliminate the effect of disturbing factor and enhance the sensitivity and specificity. However, they are not impressive than that in some other study (Chandra, Mahto, Chowdhuri, Das, & Sahu, 2017; J. Li, Li, & Liu, 2019). The reason might be use of single and down-conversion nanomaterials which were interfered by autofluorescence of bacterial cells (Li, Ahmad, Rong, Chen, Zuo, Ouyang, et al., 2020). There are some strategies to improve this sensitivity. For example, excellent nanocomposites based on CQDs can be further investigated to improve anti-disturbing ability. And the prepared CQDs can couple with immunoassays to improve sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the fluorescent probe was investigated by five different strains which were common in milk samples (Lb. acidophilus, Lc. lactis, B. cereus, S. aureus, and E. coli). Each bacterial cells were incubated with CQDs-MNPs probe for 40 min. After magnetic separation, the fluorescent intensity of CQDs-MNPs was measured for each strain (Fig. 6 ). The fluorescent intensity of the four strains (Lb. acidophilus, Lc. lactis, B. cereus, and S. aureus) was in agreement with value obtained for the blank samples. The fluorescent intensity value with E. coli was much lower than the values obtained for other four strains. It indicates that only the presence of E. coli can cause a decrease in fluorescent intensity. Interference was not generated in the presence of Lb. acidophilus, Lc. lactis, B. cereus, and S. aureus. That might be attributed to the specificity of aptamer to E. coli cells(Díaz-Amaya, Lin, Deering, & Stanciu, 2019). The CQDs-MNPs stored for one month was employed for E. coli detection to evaluate their stability. The fluorescent intensities of fresh and stored CQDs-MNPs in the presence of E. coli with 104 CFU/mL were compared, and the CV values of them were calculated. The FL intensity values were 341, 352, 360, 385, 395 and 350 a.u. with CV of 5.9% using the stored CQDs-MNPs. The FL intensity values were 408, 353, 368, 386, 405 and 411 a.u with CV of 6.1% using the fresh CQDs-MNPs. The CV of all FL intensity values was 6.7%. Therefore, the CV value of fluorescent intensity using the fresh and stored CQDs was less than 6.7%. These results show that the CQDs-MNPs probe exhibits good selectivity and stability toward E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of E. Coli in milk samples stored for different time
ce:section.ce:para: Milk samples under different storage time (0, 8, 16, 20, and 24 h) were prepared to evaluate the accuracy of this fluorescent method. Each kind of milk sample had seven parallel samples (n = 7). Sample extracting solutions from milk samples were assayed by the fluorescent method. As shown in Table S3, the E. coli concentration in milk samples stored for 0, 8, 16, 20, and 24 h was (5.1 ± 0.14) × 102, (8.6 ± 0.11) × 102, (4.5 ± 0.12) × 103, (6.4 ± 0.08) × 104, and (7.2 ± 0.05) × 105 CFU/mL, respectively. The concentration of E. coli was increased with lengthening of storage time. The coefficients of variation (CV) of them were 5.6%, 5.1%, 4.8%, 4.5%, and 4.9%. The performance of the fluorescent method was consistent with that obtained by the plate-counting method ((5.2 ± 0.11) × 102, (8.1 ± 0.13) × 102, (4.3 ± 0.15) × 103, (6.5 ± 0.10) × 104, and (7.9 ± 0.07) × 105 with less than CV of 5.7%. No significant difference was found between this method and the standard method (P > 0.05). The practical results prove that this method can recognize and quantify E. coli in real samples using aptamer-CQDs labeling and cDNA-MNPs separation.

CQDs combined with magnetic nanoparticles was successfully fabricated for sensitive and specific detection of E. coli. The synthetic method of CQDs with orange peel as carbon sources was simple, efficient, green and low-cost. The CQDs displayed bright green fluorescence with an average size of 4.2 nm. CQDs-MNPs was formed as a fluorescent probe to recognize and quantify E. coli based on aptamer-CQDs and cDNA-MNPs. A fluorescent method for E. coli was developed based on the linear relationship between fluorescent signal and E. coli concentration (R2 = 0.977) with low limit of detection (487 CFU/mL). Milk samples contaminated by E. coli were assayed by the fluorescent method and the performance agreed with that detected by plate-counting methods. In summary, the study provides a novel way to synthesize CQDs and an efficient method to assay E. coli.
Informed consent is not applicable for the nature of this study.

Xuetao Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yanxiao Li: Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yiwei Xu: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation. Ziyu Gan: Methodology, Investigation. Xiaobo Zou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Jiyong Shi: Software, Writing - review & editing. Xiaowei Huang: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Zhihua Li: Methodology, Investigation. Yahui Li: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31671844, 31601543), National Key Technology Research and Development Program of China (2018YFD0400803, 2017YFC1600805, 2017YFC1600806) and Six Talent Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province (GDZB-016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127775.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
